spec_match.rationale: Pay button calls real PaymentRequest.show() against the same-origin handler, then branches on err.name to either show a cancel message or surface a fallback method — the spec's intended pattern.

interactive.rationale: Outcome <select>, real Pay button, conditional fallback method button, and a reset flow that re-runs the whole loop. Real handler under the hood.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises gentle nudge for cancellation and a fallback path for internal errors — both render, with the fallback button appearing only when the OperationError branch is taken.

edge_cases.rationale: Three outcomes are wired up but the fallback path is a single retry that always succeeds in the demo. No exploration of `fallback also errors` (double-OperationError) or `user cancels the fallback`. The page also doesn't probe user-activation re-acquisition (which Chrome requires for the second show()).

design_system.rationale: Page leans on emoji and glyph characters across the UI: `💳`, `🏦`, `⟳`, `✓ ✕ ⚠`. Project rule is inline SVG, never emoji, for UI elements — this is a clear regression.

openQuestions.title: Replace emoji and glyph icons with inline SVG
openQuestions.detail: Payment method buttons (`💳 Card`, `🏦 Bank transfer`), the retry spinner `⟳`, and the outcome titles `✓ ✕ ⚠` all use emoji/unicode glyphs. Project convention is inline SVG with `currentColor` so colour, accessibility, and dark-mode all behave. Replace each.

openQuestions.title: Exercise fallback-also-errors path
openQuestions.detail: The fallback retry currently always succeeds. Add a `fallback also fails` scenario so the merchant code's second-level catch is observable. This is exactly the case where developers tend to introduce infinite retry loops — worth demoing the correct cap.

openQuestions.title: Show the user-activation re-acquisition requirement
openQuestions.detail: After OperationError, Chrome requires a fresh user gesture before show() can be called again. The page silently triggers the second show() in the same JS turn — that works because of the timer, but the convention is wrong. Make the user click `Try fallback method` and surface the rule.

openQuestions.title: Persist basket state across the retry
openQuestions.detail: The lede mentions `your basket is still saved`, but the page state is in-memory. A localStorage / sessionStorage round-trip would back up the prose with code worth copying.

openQuestions.title: Surface err.message alongside the name
openQuestions.detail: The log-trace prints `err.name === 'OperationError' → true` but not the message. The whole point of the structured error is that the message carries the detail; show it.

summary: Concept is good and the handler/branching code is real, but emoji UI icons and an unconditionally-succeeding fallback path leave the page weaker than its siblings. Replace the glyphs with SVG and stress the fallback-also-fails branch to round it out.
